result: There’s a quiet thrill in finding the last clean mug in the office cupboard at 7:53 a.m., the one that isn’t chipped or branded with a half-peeled tech-conference sticker. I rinse it anyway, out of habit, watching yesterday’s coffee swirl clockwise down the drain—the same direction the milk spins in my cereal bowl, the same direction my thoughts spiral when the day hasn’t yet asked anything of me. Outside, a city bus exhales like an old teacher disappointed by the essay I never turned in, and I forgive us both. Inside my headphones, a woman sings in a language I don’t speak, but the chorus sounds exactly like the word “almost,” stretched until it becomes a place you could live in. I could stay there, paying rent in unfinished sentences, harvesting parentheses from the garden no one else remembers planting. Instead I lock the apartment door twice—once for security, once for symmetry—and join the march of coats that believe their destinations more than their bodies do. Somewhere on the sixth floor, a spreadsheet is warming up like a cathedral organ, waiting for me to pull out every stop. I practice my chords of canned replies, my scales of small talk, but the mug keeps radiating heat against my palm, a private sun, reminding me that gravity is just one opinion and the day is still unsigned, a rough draft steaming on the windowsill, reachable.

Inside my headphones, a woman sings in a language I don’t speak, but the chorus sounds exactly like the word “almost,” stretched until it becomes a place you could live in. I could stay there, paying rent in unfinished sentences, harvesting parentheses from the garden no one else remembers planting. Instead I lock the apartment door twice—once for security, once for symmetry—and join the march of coats that believe their destinations more than their bodies do. Somewhere on the sixth floor, a spreadsheet is warming up like a cathedral organ, waiting for me to pull out every stop. I practice my chords of canned replies, my scales of small talk, but the mug keeps radiating heat against my palm, a private sun, reminding me that gravity is just one opinion and the day is still unsigned, a rough draft steaming on the windowsill, reachable.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
